title: Claude Mythos 5 + Fable 5 Are Here And The Numbers Are INSANE
summary: Anthropic released Claude Mythos 5 and Fable 5 (Mythos 5 with guardrails) with strong performance metrics. Fable 5 is available at no extra cost on paid subscription plans through June 22, after which access requires usage credits.
writeup: This video breaks down Anthropic's release of Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5, where Fable 5 is the Mythos-class model made generally available with classifier-based guardrails, while Mythos 5 ships without those guardrails to a small subset of users (a cybersecurity pilot called Glass Wing). The presenter works through pricing ($10/M input, $50/M output tokens), benchmark jumps over Opus 4.8, real-world deployment results from Stripe, and the classifier routing system that falls back to Opus 4.8 for risky queries in fewer than 5% of sessions. The through-line is that Fable 5 is a genuine architectural step forward rather than an incremental revision, with the safeguard layer being the substantive design decision separating the two releases.
key_points: Fable 5 is priced at $10 per million input tokens and $50 per million output tokens, less than half the cost of the Mythos preview but double Opus 4.8, and the presenter notes token efficiency gains may soften the per-project cost increase.

On SWE-Bench Pro, Fable 5 scores 80% versus 69% for Opus 4.8, and on Agentic Coding the gap is 29.3 versus 13.4, representing the largest benchmark jump the presenter has seen between consecutive Claude generations.
Stripe used Fable 5 to perform a codebase-wide migration of a 50-million-line Ruby codebase in a single day, a task the team estimated would take over two months of manual engineering work.

The classifier routing system detects queries touching cybersecurity, biology, chemistry, and distillation and automatically routes them to Opus 4.8 instead of Fable 5, with fallback occurring in fewer than 5% of sessions and the model disclosing the switch to the user.

On a Firefox offensive-cyber benchmark, Mythos 5 succeeds 88.4% of the time while Fable 5 scores zero across the board, a result of the classifier blocking those queries entirely; a concurrent external bug bounty found no universal jailbreaks in over 1,000 hours of testing.

At the effort-level cost curve for Fable 5, accuracy gains flatten sharply moving from extra-high to max effort while cost spikes from roughly $12 to $20, making extra-high the practical sweet spot for most coding tasks.
